chapter: Chapter 1: The First March on Rome (88-87 BCE)

questions.question: Why was the Mithridatic command so desirable to Roman generals? (SELECT ALL THAT APPLY)
options: It offered opportunities for massive plunder from wealthy eastern provinces
The Senate had pledged to double the commander's land grants upon return
Roman religious law required blood vengeance for the massacre of citizens abroad
Success would bring unmatched military glory and political influence
Eastern campaigns historically lasted long enough to build deep personal loyalty among soldiers

questions.question: What characterized Sulla's background before his rise to military prominence? (SELECT ALL THAT APPLY)
options: He came from an ancient patrician family that had fallen into obscurity
He was known for consorting with actors and theatrical performers
He had served as tribune of the plebs and championed popular reform causes
He distinguished himself early through a brilliant independent campaign in Spain
He spent his youth in relative poverty, living in a cheap insula
He rose to prominence through generous patronage of the urban poor

questions.question: What made Sulla's march on Rome possible according to the chapter? (SELECT ALL THAT APPLY)
options: The Senate had secretly authorized the use of force against Marius
Common soldiers felt no loyalty to traditions they barely understood
Religious authorities had granted a special exemption from the pomerium
Marius had disbanded the city garrison, leaving Rome effectively defenseless
The military reforms had created professional soldiers loyal to their generals
His soldiers expected him to lead them to plunder in the East

questions.question: What forms of resistance did defenders attempt against Sulla's march on Rome? (SELECT ALL THAT APPLY)
options: Organizing gladiators and armed slaves to fight the advancing legions
Romans throwing tiles and debris from rooftops at soldiers in the streets
Summoning allied Italian armies stationed in the surrounding countryside
The tribunes invoking their sacred veto to halt the army's advance
Fortifying the city gates and preparing for a prolonged siege

questions.question: What factors explain Sulla's relative restraint after his first capture of Rome? (SELECT ALL THAT APPLY)
options: He saw himself as defending constitutional order, not overthrowing it
He needed to depart quickly for the war against Mithridates
The Senate threatened to declare him a public enemy if he continued
He limited executions to roughly a dozen opponents rather than conducting mass killings
He secretly negotiated a power-sharing agreement with Marius
His soldiers refused to carry out further violence against fellow citizens

questions.question: How did Cinna build his forces after being expelled from Rome? (SELECT ALL THAT APPLY)
options: He recruited the army still besieging Social War rebels at Nola
He summoned Marius back from exile in Africa
New Italian citizens denied their promised voting rights flocked to his banner
He hired Germanic mercenaries with gold supplied by eastern allies
Slaves were promised freedom in exchange for military service
He negotiated an alliance with Mithridates who sent warships and troops

questions.question: What characterized the Marian massacres of late 87 BCE? (SELECT ALL THAT APPLY)
options: Marius's slave-soldiers, the Bardyiae, roamed streets killing identified opponents
A nod or refused handshake from Marius could serve as a death sentence
The consul Octavius was killed in his curule chair and his head publicly displayed
The killings were limited to senators who had openly supported Sulla's march
Cinna organized the massacres through formal judicial proceedings and public trials
The violence lasted approximately three weeks before order was restored

questions.question: What precedents did the events of 88-87 BCE establish for Roman politics? (SELECT ALL THAT APPLY)
options: Military force could be used to settle domestic political disputes
Political violence had escalated from individual murder to systematic massacre
Soldiers' loyalty to their generals proved stronger than loyalty to the Republic
Exile was no longer a permanent solution to political enemies
The Senate permanently lost all constitutional authority over military commands
The tribunate was formally abolished as a political office
questions.textReference: Conclusion: The Point of No Return

questions.question: What was the state of Rome during Cinna's dominance from 86-84 BCE? (SELECT ALL THAT APPLY)
options: Cinna held the consulship continuously from 87 to 84 BCE in defiance of term limits
Rome functioned as a military dictatorship despite maintaining republican forms
The regime was sustained by force and the anticipation of Sulla's inevitable return
Cinna governed through genuine consensus and broad senatorial support
The Senate formally abolished the consulship and granted Cinna royal authority
Cinna negotiated a permanent peace treaty with Sulla recognizing divided spheres of rule

questions.question: What transformation had occurred in Marius by the time he returned from exile? (SELECT ALL THAT APPLY)
options: He was consumed by desire for vengeance after months of humiliation and flight
His exile had broken him psychologically, leading to murderous depression upon his return
He had developed a sophisticated strategy for political reconciliation with the Senate
He returned with a large professional army recruited from African kingdoms
He had forged a military alliance with Mithridates to coordinate attacks on Sulla
